sentence: Never I have seen such a beautiful sunset.
correct: Never have I seen such a beautiful sunset.
hint: Check the word order after 'Never'
explanation: Negative adverbs at the start of a sentence require subject-auxiliary inversion: 'Never have I seen'.

sentence: Not until she spoke I realised who she was.
correct: Not until she spoke did I realise who she was.
Not until she spoke did I realize who she was.
hint: Check the word order in the main clause after 'Not until'
explanation: 'Not until' triggers inversion in the main clause: 'did I realise', not 'I realised'.

sentence: Under no circumstances you should reveal this information.
correct: Under no circumstances should you reveal this information.
hint: Check the word order after 'Under no circumstances'
explanation: Negative fronted adverbials require inversion: 'should you reveal'.

sentence: Only after the meeting finished we understood the problem.
correct: Only after the meeting finished did we understand the problem.
hint: Check the word order in the main clause
explanation: 'Only after' triggers inversion in the main clause: 'did we understand'.

sentence: Little they knew about the dangers ahead.
correct: Little did they know about the dangers ahead.
hint: Check the word order after 'Little'
explanation: 'Little' as a negative adverb at the start requires inversion: 'Little did they know'.

sentence: It is essential that he arrives on time.
correct: It is essential that he arrive on time.
hint: Check the verb form in the that-clause
explanation: The subjunctive mood after 'essential that' requires the base form: 'arrive', not 'arrives'.

sentence: The committee demanded that the report is submitted by Friday.
correct: The committee demanded that the report be submitted by Friday.
hint: Check the verb form in the that-clause
explanation: The subjunctive after 'demanded that' requires 'be submitted', not 'is submitted'.

sentence: I suggest that she takes a different approach.
correct: I suggest that she take a different approach.
hint: Check the verb form after 'suggest that'
explanation: The subjunctive after 'suggest that' requires the base form: 'take', not 'takes'.

sentence: It is vital that every student understands the rules.
correct: It is vital that every student understand the rules.
hint: Check the verb form in the that-clause
explanation: The subjunctive after 'vital that' requires the base form: 'understand', not 'understands'.

sentence: The doctor recommended that he takes the medication daily.
correct: The doctor recommended that he take the medication daily.
hint: Check the verb form after 'recommended that'
explanation: The subjunctive after 'recommended that' requires the base form: 'take', not 'takes'.

sentence: She came up the idea during the brainstorming session.
correct: She came up with the idea during the brainstorming session.
hint: Check the phrasal verb particle
explanation: The correct phrasal verb is 'come up with' (an idea), not 'come up'.

sentence: He turned up to be a very talented musician.
correct: He turned out to be a very talented musician.
hint: Check the phrasal verb
explanation: 'Turn out to be' means 'prove to be'. 'Turn up' means 'arrive' or 'appear'.

sentence: The company has been putting up with the losses for too long and decided to cut off some departments.
correct: The company has been putting up with the losses for too long and decided to cut back on some departments.
hint: Check the second phrasal verb
explanation: 'Cut off' means 'disconnect'. 'Cut back on' means 'reduce' — which fits the context of reducing departments.

sentence: We need to look into this matter before we can get on a conclusion.
correct: We need to look into this matter before we can come to a conclusion.
hint: Check the phrasal verb with 'conclusion'
explanation: The correct collocation is 'come to a conclusion', not 'get on a conclusion'.

sentence: She broke up the news to him very gently.
correct: She broke the news to him very gently.
hint: Check the phrasal verb
explanation: 'Break the news' (deliver bad news) does not use 'up'. 'Break up' means 'end a relationship'.

sentence: Rarely the government has taken such drastic measures.
correct: Rarely has the government taken such drastic measures.
hint: Check the word order after 'Rarely'
explanation: 'Rarely' at the start requires inversion: 'Rarely has the government taken'.

sentence: At no time the suspect was seen near the building.
correct: At no time was the suspect seen near the building.
hint: Check the word order after 'At no time'
explanation: 'At no time' requires inversion: 'was the suspect seen'.

sentence: It is imperative that the project is completed on schedule.
correct: It is imperative that the project be completed on schedule.
hint: Check the verb form in the that-clause
explanation: The subjunctive after 'imperative that' requires 'be completed', not 'is completed'.

sentence: He ran into with an old friend at the conference.
correct: He ran into an old friend at the conference.
hint: Check the phrasal verb
explanation: 'Run into' (meet by chance) does not take 'with'. Remove 'with'.

sentence: So serious was the situation that the president called up an emergency meeting.
correct: So serious was the situation that the president called an emergency meeting.
hint: Check the phrasal verb with 'meeting'
explanation: 'Call a meeting' is the correct collocation. 'Call up' means 'phone someone' or 'summon for military service'.

sentence: In no way this decision should affect your daily routine.
correct: In no way should this decision affect your daily routine.
hint: Check the word order after 'In no way'
explanation: 'In no way' requires inversion: 'should this decision affect'.

sentence: The board insisted that the CEO resigns immediately.
correct: The board insisted that the CEO resign immediately.
hint: Check the verb form after 'insisted that'
explanation: The subjunctive after 'insisted that' requires the base form: 'resign', not 'resigns'.

sentence: She put off with his rude behaviour for years.
correct: She put up with his rude behaviour for years.
hint: Check the phrasal verb meaning 'tolerate'
explanation: 'Put up with' means 'tolerate'. 'Put off' means 'postpone'.

sentence: Only when the results were announced the students celebrated.
correct: Only when the results were announced did the students celebrate.
hint: Check the word order in the main clause
explanation: 'Only when' triggers inversion in the main clause: 'did the students celebrate'.

sentence: It is crucial that the data remains confidential at all times.
correct: It is crucial that the data remain confidential at all times.
hint: Check the verb form in the that-clause
explanation: The subjunctive after 'crucial that' requires the base form: 'remain', not 'remains'.

sentence: Not only he failed the test, but he also lost his scholarship.
correct: Not only did he fail the test, but he also lost his scholarship.
hint: Check the word order after 'Not only'
explanation: 'Not only' at the start requires inversion: 'Not only did he fail'.

sentence: She looked down at her colleagues because of their lack of experience.
correct: She looked down on her colleagues because of their lack of experience.
hint: Check the phrasal verb particle
explanation: 'Look down on' means 'regard with contempt'. 'Look down at' means physically looking downward.
